title: Informal Conjecture (generalization of 1.3 to bounded clique number)
statement_text: The corresponding result to 1.3 is true for graphs with bounded clique number rather than just triangle-free graphs: for all integers $\nu > 0$ and $k \geq 3$ there exists $n$ such that if $G$ has no clique of cardinality $k$ and has chromatic number at least $n$, then for some $t$, $G$ has a hole of length $t+i$ for $1 \leq i \leq \nu$.
context_text: Immediately after the statement of the main result (1.3), which establishes $\nu$ consecutive hole lengths for triangle-free graphs, the authors conjecture the same conclusion holds under the weaker assumption that $G$ has bounded clique number. They note they have made no progress in proving this.
notes: PDF source — exact statement inferred from prose; the phrase 'the corresponding result' refers to Theorem 1.3 in context
paper_title: Induced subgraphs of graphs with large chromatic number. IV. Consecutive holes

title: Conjecture 1.5
statement_text: Let $F$ be an infinite set of positive integers with bounded gaps. Then $F$ is constricting.
context_text: A set $F$ of integers is $k$-constricting if there exists $n$ such that every graph with chromatic number at least $n$ contains either a clique with $k$ vertices or a hole with length in $F$; $F$ is constricting if it is $k$-constricting for every $k$. The authors prove (1.4) that every infinite set with bounded gaps is 3-constricting, and conjecture 1.5 as the natural generalization to all $k$.
notes: PDF source — math appears cleanly readable
paper_title: Induced subgraphs of graphs with large chromatic number. IV. Consecutive holes

title: Problem 1.6
statement_text: Is there an infinite set $F$ of positive integers such that $F$ is constricting and has density $0$?
context_text: The authors discuss which infinite sets are constricting, noting that not every infinite set is constricting (not even sets with upper density one), while sets with bounded gaps are 3-constricting. Lower density seems closer to the truth, and the authors conjecture the answer to Problem 1.6 is positive.
notes: PDF source — math appears cleanly readable; authors conjecture the answer is positive
paper_title: Induced subgraphs of graphs with large chromatic number. IV. Consecutive holes

title: Problem 1.7
statement_text: Is there an infinite set $F = \{f_1, f_2, \ldots\}$ of positive integers such that $F$ is constricting and $f_{i+1} - f_i \to \infty$ as $i \to \infty$?
context_text: This is a strengthening of Problem 1.6; an affirmative answer would show that a constricting set can have gaps tending to infinity. The authors note it would be very interesting to answer both Problems 1.6 and 1.7 even in the weaker case of 3-constricting sets.
notes: PDF source — math appears cleanly readable
paper_title: Induced subgraphs of graphs with large chromatic number. IV. Consecutive holes

title: Informal Question (on 2.1 for ℓ = 4)
statement_text: Statement 2.1 might be true when $\ell = 4$: let $\varphi : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ be a non-decreasing function; does there exist $n$ such that every $(2,\varphi)$-controlled triangle-free graph with chromatic number more than $n$ has a $4$-hole?
context_text: Statement 2.1 is proved in the paper for all $\ell \geq 5$ (with $\ell = 7$ handled by a prior paper). The authors remark that 2.1 might also hold for $\ell = 4$ but that they have not been able to decide this.
notes: PDF source — phrased by authors as 'might be true … but we have not been able to decide this'; no formal conjecture label
paper_title: Induced subgraphs of graphs with large chromatic number. IV. Consecutive holes
